companies.name: Apple
companies.notes: Publishes twice a year, covering request counts by country and type — push token requests, geofence warrants, device data. Has done this since 2013.

companies.name: Google / Fitbit
companies.notes: Fitbit has been under Google's transparency report since the 2021 acquisition. Google's government disclosures grew 557% between 2014 and 2025, per Proton's analysis of Google Transparency Report data.

companies.name: Garmin
companies.notes: Has an [intake form for law enforcement](https://www.garmin.com/en-US/forms/lawenforcement/) but publishes no data on how many requests it receives or what it does with them.

companies.name: Oura
companies.notes: Confirmed to [Zack Whitaker](https://this.weekinsecurity.com/oura-says-it-gets-government-demands-for-user-data-will-it-share-how-many/) that it gets government requests, but as of May 2026 won't say how many or how often it complies. A spokesperson said in late 2025 it was "actively evaluating" disclosure. Health data is not end-to-end encrypted. Previously tied to a [since-cancelled $96M US military contract](https://www.biometricupdate.com/202503/dod-unexpectedly-cancels-controversial-wearable-biometric-contract).

companies.name: WHOOP
companies.notes: Flagged in a [2025 peer-reviewed study](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-025-01757-1) of 17 wearable makers as exhibiting substantive deficiencies in privacy protection — the lowest-scoring cluster in the study. A class-action lawsuit filed the same year alleged it was secretly sharing health data with third-party trackers.

companies.name: Samsung
companies.notes: [Ranking Digital Rights](https://rankingdigitalrights.org/index2019/companies/samsung/index/) found in 2019 that Samsung disclosed nothing about government requests, and noted no legal reason it couldn't. No transparency report found as of May 2026.

companies.name: Polar
companies.notes: Rated mid-tier in a [2025 study](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-025-01757-1) of 17 wearable makers' privacy practices. No law enforcement disclosures found.

companies.name: Coros
companies.notes: Incorporated in California but operationally based in Dongguan, China. Rated medium risk in a [2025 privacy study](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-025-01757-1) of 17 wearable makers. No government data disclosures found.

companies.name: Suunto
companies.notes: Owned by Liesheng Electronic Technology (China) since May 2022, though still headquartered in Vantaa, Finland and operating under EU law. Fell into the lowest-scoring cluster in a [2025 academic review](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-025-01757-1) of wearable privacy practices, alongside WHOOP and Ultrahuman. No government data disclosures found.

companies.name: Ultrahuman
companies.notes: Also in the lowest-rated tier in the [same 2025 study](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-025-01757-1). Its privacy policy states that user data, including email addresses, is used for third-party advertising.

companies.name: Amazfit / Zepp
companies.notes: Zepp Health relocated its legal entity to Amsterdam and its principal executive offices to Gorinchem, Netherlands in May 2025 (previously Hefei, China). Manufacturing and operations remain China-based; the company retains ties to Xiaomi. The [privacy policy](https://us.amazfit.com/pages/privacy-policy) states that national security requests can result in data disclosure and that data may be shared with Xiaomi. [Mozilla's review](https://www.mozillafoundation.org/en/privacynotincluded/amazfit-fitness-trackers/) flags multiple concerns.

companies.name: Withings
companies.notes: Makes medically-focused hardware (ECG, blood pressure) but no government data disclosures found. Rated mid-tier in a [2025 study](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-025-01757-1) of wearable privacy practices.
